content:
[Git Commit Message Guidelines > Table of Contents]

- [Why Good Commit Messages Matter](#why-good-commit-messages-matter)
- [Commit Granularity](#commit-granularity-keep-commits-focused)
- [Commit Message Structure](#commit-message-structure-conventional-commits)
- [Tooling for Consistency](#tooling-for-consistency-commitizen-interactive-cli)
- [Enforcing Standards](#optional-enforcing-standards-with-commitlint)
- [Summary](#summary)

[Git Commit Message Guidelines > Why Good Commit Messages Matter]

Good commit messages are essential for:

✨ **Speed up understanding**

- Quick grasp of changes without reading code diff
- Context for future reference

🔍 **Facilitate debugging**

- Easy bug tracking with `git bisect`
- Clear change history

👥 **Improve collaboration**

- Streamlined code reviews
- Better team communication

🤖 **Enable automation**

- Automatic changelog generation
- Simplified version management

[Git Commit Message Guidelines > Commit Granularity: Keep Commits Focused]

> **Golden Rule**: Make small, atomic commits that represent a single logical change.

[Git Commit Message Guidelines > Commit Granularity: Keep Commits Focused > What constitutes a "single logical change"?]

- 🐛 One specific bug fix
- ✨ One self-contained feature
- 🔄 One focused refactoring task
- 📝 One documentation update

[Git Commit Message Guidelines > Commit Granularity: Keep Commits Focused > Benefits of Atomic Commits]

| Benefit             | Description                                   |
| ------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Easier Reviews      | Smaller diffs = faster, more accurate reviews |
| Better History      | Clear step-by-step project evolution          |
| Simple Reverts      | Easy to undo specific changes                 |
| Effective Debugging | More precise `git bisect` results             |

[Git Commit Message Guidelines > Commit Message Structure: Conventional Commits > Basic Structure]

<type>[optional scope]: <description>

    [optional body]

    [optional footer(s)]

[Git Commit Message Guidelines > Commit Message Structure: Conventional Commits > Components]

| Component   | Required | Description                              |
| ----------- | -------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Type        | ✅       | Category of change (e.g., `feat`, `fix`) |
| Scope       | ❌       | Context (e.g., module name)              |
| Description | ✅       | Short summary in imperative mood         |
| Body        | ❌       | Detailed explanation of changes          |
| Footer      | ❌       | Metadata (breaking changes, issue refs)  |

[Git Commit Message Guidelines > Commit Message Structure: Conventional Commits > Commit Types Reference]

| Type       | Description              |
| ---------- | ------------------------ |
| `feat`     | New feature addition     |
| `fix`      | Bug fix                  |
| `chore`    | Maintenance tasks        |
| `docs`     | Documentation updates    |
| `style`    | Code formatting changes  |
| `refactor` | Code restructuring       |
| `perf`     | Performance improvements |
| `test`     | Test-related changes     |
| `build`    | Build system changes     |
| `ci`       | CI configuration updates |
| `revert`   | Commit reversal          |

[Git Commit Message Guidelines > Tooling for Consistency: Commitizen (Interactive CLI) > Installation]

# npm
    npm install --save-dev commitizen cz-conventional-changelog

    # yarn
    yarn add --dev commitizen cz-conventional-changelog

[Git Commit Message Guidelines > Tooling for Consistency: Commitizen (Interactive CLI) > Usage Flow]

1. Stage changes: `git add <files>`
2. Create commit: `git cz` or `npm run commit`
3. Follow interactive prompts

[Git Commit Message Guidelines > Optional: Enforcing Standards with Commitlint > Quick Setup]

# Installation
    npm install --save-dev @commitlint/cli @commitlint/config-conventional husky

    # Configure commitlint
    echo "module.exports = {extends: ['@commitlint/config-conventional']};" > commitlint.config.js

    # Setup Husky
    npx husky install
    npx husky add .husky/commit-msg 'npx --no -- commitlint --edit "$1"'

[Git Commit Message Guidelines > Summary]

✅ **Best Practices**

- Create small, focused commits
- Follow Conventional Commits format
- Use Commitizen for consistency
- Consider Commitlint for enforcement

🚫 **Avoid**

- Mixing unrelated changes
- Vague commit messages
- Breaking conventional format

---

_For more detailed information about Git commit conventions, visit [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/)._

[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation]

**Environment:** preprod-jp
**Date:** March 2026
**Status:** Resolved

---

[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 1. The Problem > What Was Observed]

All 4 dashboard widgets on the `sentinel-tickets-dashboard` in the `preprod-jp` environment displayed **"Oops, something went wrong."** when tests were run via Playwright Test automation (`npx playwright test`). The affected widgets were:
- Pie Chart
- Ticket Count Metric
- Column Bar
- Table

The tests in `tests/platform/dashboards/ticketsDashboard.test.ts` were failing because the metric widget's numeric value never appeared, the widgets never rendered, and after retries, the tests timed out and reported failures.

[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 1. The Problem > What Made It Confusing]

The issue did NOT reproduce in any of the following scenarios:
- Manual browsing to the same dashboard
- Browsing via Chrome in incognito mode
- Using `playwright-cli` to navigate to the dashboard
- Running the same tests against the QA environment

This meant it was **not a general backend outage**, **not an authentication problem**, and **not a browser incompatibility**. The failure was uniquely isolated to Playwright Test automation against preprod-jp.

[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 1. The Problem > Reported Context]

The tests had been passing before. No changes were made to `playwright.config.ts`, Playwright version, or dependencies. The failure appeared suddenly, which strongly suggested an **external change** — either a DevRev frontend deployment, a backend change, or a Chrome auto-update.

---

[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 2. How the App Loads Dashboard Data (Background)]

Understanding the architecture of how the DevRev dashboard loads widget data is critical to understanding this bug.

When a dashboard page loads, it follows this sequence:

```
1. dashboards.get       → Fetches the dashboard definition (widget configs, layout)
2. oasis.data.fetch (×3) → Fetches metadata for each dataset (returns parquet file download URLs + auth tokens)
3. oasis.data.stream (×3) → Downloads the actual parquet data files (from CDN)
4. DuckDB-WASM           → Processes the parquet data in-browser using SQL
5. Widget renders        → Displays the computed result (chart, metric value, table rows)
```

The DevRev dashboard uses **DuckDB-WASM** — an in-browser SQL database compiled to WebAssembly. It downloads raw data as [Parquet](https://parquet.apache.org/) files, loads them into DuckDB, and runs SQL queries locally in the browser. This avoids round-trips to a server for every query.

There is a **server-side fallback**: `oasis.data.query`. If the client-side DuckDB path fails for any reason, the app falls back to sending the SQL to the server. The server executes the query and returns results.

---

metadata.section: 2. How the App Loads Dashboard Data (Background)
headingHierarchy: 2. How the App Loads Dashboard Data (Background)

content:
[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 3. Root Cause Chain]

Through trace analysis and network inspection, we confirmed the following sequence of failures:

```
Step 1: oasis.data.fetch (×3)     → ✅ 200 OK — metadata loaded fine
Step 2: DuckDB-WASM initialization → ❌ SKIPPED — never even starts
Step 3: oasis.data.stream          → Only 0–1 requests fire (instead of 3)
Step 4: oasis.data.query (×5)      → ❌ 500 Internal Server Error
Step 5: React error boundary       → Catches exception, shows "Oops, something went wrong."
```

There are **two separate bugs** interacting here:

metadata.section: Bug #1 (Primary) — DuckDB-WASM disabled for automated browsers
headingHierarchy: Bug #1 (Primary) — DuckDB-WASM disabled for automated browsers

content:
[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 3. Root Cause Chain > Bug #2 (Secondary) — oasis.data.query returns 500 in preprod-jp]

The server-side fallback endpoint (`oasis.data.query`) has a bug in the preprod-jp environment — it returns HTTP 500 for all queries. This is a **backend bug unrelated to our automation** but it compounds the problem fatally: when DuckDB is bypassed, the only remaining path is broken.

If Bug #2 were fixed (server returns 200), the widgets would still work even with DuckDB disabled. If Bug #1 were fixed (DuckDB initialized), the fallback would never be called. Either fix independently would restore widget functionality — but only Bug #1 is within our control.

---

[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 4. Why playwright-cli Worked]

`playwright-cli` is a developer tool for browser automation that is built on Playwright internally. The key difference is in **how it connects to the browser**:

- **Playwright Test** injects the `--enable-automation` Chrome flag and sets `navigator.webdriver = true` via the CDP automation protocol — standard behavior for headless testing frameworks.
- **playwright-cli** connects to Chrome as a developer/inspection tool. It does **not** inject the `--enable-automation` flag and does **not** set `navigator.webdriver`. From the app's perspective, it is indistinguishable from a regular user session.

This is why `playwright-cli` consistently showed the DuckDB view creation logs in its console:
```
[INFO] View Query: CREATE or REPLACE VIEW devrev.dim_ticket as SELECT * from read_parquet(['devrev.dim_ticket_?token=...'])
[INFO] View Query: CREATE or REPLACE VIEW devrev.dim_revo as SELECT * from read_parquet([...])
[INFO] View Query: CREATE or REPLACE VIEW devrev.dim_sla_tracker as SELECT * from read_parquet([...])
```

These logs were **completely absent** in Playwright Test runs, confirming DuckDB was never initialized.

---

[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 5. Investigation Journey — What We Tried]

We conducted a systematic isolation exercise. Here is every approach attempted, in order, and why each was tried.

---

[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 5. Investigation Journey — What We Tried > Attempt 1: Rule out the QA vs preprod-jp environment difference]

**Hypothesis:** Maybe the test itself is broken, not the environment.
**Test:** Ran the exact same test suite against QA environment.
**Result:** ✅ All tests passed in QA.
**Conclusion:** The issue is preprod-jp specific. The test code is fine.

---

metadata.section: Attempt 1: Rule out the QA vs preprod-jp environment difference
headingHierarchy: Attempt 1: Rule out the QA vs preprod-jp environment difference

content:
[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 5. Investigation Journey — What We Tried > Attempt 2: Rule out authentication as the cause]

**Hypothesis:** Maybe the auth cookies are stale or incorrect.
**Test:** Manually browsed to the dashboard in preprod-jp using the same user account.
**Result:** ✅ Widgets loaded and displayed data correctly.
**Conclusion:** Auth is fine. The issue is specific to the automated browser context.

---

metadata.section: Attempt 2: Rule out authentication as the cause
headingHierarchy: Attempt 2: Rule out authentication as the cause

content:
[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 5. Investigation Journey — What We Tried > Attempt 3: Rule out browser caching/localStorage as the cause]

**Hypothesis:** Maybe the dashboard needs something from localStorage or cache to initialize DuckDB.
**Test:** Opened the dashboard in a fresh Chrome incognito window (no cache, no localStorage).
**Result:** ✅ Widgets loaded fine in incognito.
**Conclusion:** Cache and localStorage are not required. The issue is in how Playwright launches/configures the browser.

---

metadata.section: Attempt 3: Rule out browser caching/localStorage as the cause
headingHierarchy: Attempt 3: Rule out browser caching/localStorage as the cause

content:
[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 5. Investigation Journey — What We Tried > Attempt 4: Verify playwright-cli reproduces the working behavior]

**Hypothesis:** playwright-cli is known to work — verify it consistently.
**Test:** Used `playwright-cli state-load` with the same storage state file, then navigated to the dashboard URL.
**Result:** ✅ All widgets loaded. Console showed DuckDB View Query logs confirming DuckDB-WASM ran successfully.
**Conclusion:** playwright-cli works reliably. The difference lies in how playwright-cli vs Playwright Test sets up the browser.

---

metadata.section: Attempt 4: Verify playwright-cli reproduces the working behavior
headingHierarchy: Attempt 4: Verify playwright-cli reproduces the working behavior

content:
[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 5. Investigation Journey — What We Tried > Attempt 5: Inspect the trace from the failing Playwright Test run]

**Hypothesis:** The trace will show what network requests differ from the working scenario.
**Test:** Analyzed the Playwright Test trace file from a failing run.
**Key findings:**
- `oasis.data.fetch` ×3 → all returned 200
- `oasis.data.stream` → only **1 request** made (not 3)
- `oasis.data.query` ×5 → all returned **500**
- DuckDB View Query console logs → **absent**
**Conclusion:** DuckDB is not initializing. The app is jumping straight to the server-side fallback after only partially attempting data download. The fallback is broken with 500 errors.

---

metadata.section: Attempt 5: Inspect the trace from the failing Playwright Test run
headingHierarchy: Attempt 5: Inspect the trace from the failing Playwright Test run

content:
[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 5. Investigation Journey — What We Tried > Attempt 6: Pre-cache DuckDB WASM extension files]

**Hypothesis:** Maybe DuckDB extensions download slowly (ICU extension is 6.2MB) and the download times out, causing a fallback. Playwright Test might have stricter network behavior.
**Test:** Downloaded the 3 DuckDB WASM extension files locally (`icu`, `json`, `parquet`). Used Playwright's `page.route()` to intercept extension download requests and serve them instantly from disk — eliminating download time entirely.
**Result:** ❌ Widgets still failed. Same error count, same 500s.
**Conclusion:** Download speed/caching is not the issue.

---

metadata.section: Attempt 6: Pre-cache DuckDB WASM extension files
headingHierarchy: Attempt 6: Pre-cache DuckDB WASM extension files

content:
[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 5. Investigation Journey — What We Tried > Attempt 7: Override the browser fixture to use launchPersistentContext]

**Hypothesis:** Playwright Test uses `chromium.launch()` + `browser.newContext()`. playwright-cli uses `launchPersistentContext`. Maybe the context creation method is the difference.
**Test:** Created `src/fixtures/browser.fixtures.ts` — a worker-scoped fixture override that replaced `chromium.launch()` with `chromium.launchPersistentContext('')`, confirmed by log output that the override was active.
**Result:** ❌ Widgets still failed. Same errors.
**Conclusion:** The context type (persistent vs isolated) is not the differentiator.

---

metadata.section: Attempt 7: Override the browser fixture to use launchPersistentContext
headingHierarchy: Attempt 7: Override the browser fixture to use launchPersistentContext

content:
[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 5. Investigation Journey — What We Tried > Attempt 8: Test with a standalone tsx script — persistent context with direct navigation]

**Hypothesis:** Maybe Playwright Test's internal machinery adds something that a standalone script doesn't. Also, maybe the persistent context itself (not the isolated `newContext()`) works.
**Test:** Wrote `debug-context.ts` — a standalone `npx tsx` script that used `launchPersistentContext('')` directly and navigated to the dashboard without going through Playwright Test's fixture/test runner machinery.
**Result:** ❌ FAILED. Persistent context in a standalone script also shows 4 error widgets.
**Key insight:** This proved the issue is NOT about Playwright Test's fixture system — even a raw script fails. Something at a deeper level (how the browser session is identified) is different.

---

metadata.section: Attempt 8: Test with a standalone tsx script — persistent context with direct navigation
headingHierarchy: Attempt 8: Test with a standalone tsx script — persistent context with direct navigation

content:
[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 5. Investigation Journey — What We Tried > Attempt 9: Test the warmup approach]

**Hypothesis:** Maybe DuckDB needs the browser to have already loaded the page once ("warmed up") before an isolated context works. Perhaps some browser-level cache or process-level state is needed.
**Test:** Modified the debug script to: (1) load the dashboard in the persistent context first and wait 20 seconds, (2) then create a `newContext()` within the same browser process and load the dashboard there.
**Result:** ❌ FAILED. Both the persistent context AND the isolated context showed 4 error widgets.
**Conclusion:** Warming up the browser doesn't help. The failure is not about browser-level caching.

---

[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 5. Investigation Journey — What We Tried > Attempt 10: A/B test — persistent context vs isolated newContext in same browser]

**Hypothesis:** Maybe it's specifically `newContext()` (isolated context) that fails, while the persistent context page itself works.
**Test:** In the same script, ran two tests sequentially: (A) navigate in the persistent context directly, (B) navigate in a `newContext()` within the same browser.
**Result:** ❌ BOTH FAILED. 4 error widgets in both cases.
**Critical insight:** Even the persistent context (which playwright-cli uses) fails in a programmatic script. This means the issue is NOT about persistent vs isolated context — it's about something that distinguishes playwright-cli's browser session from any programmatic script.

---

metadata.section: Attempt 10: A/B test — persistent context vs isolated newContext in same browser
headingHierarchy: Attempt 10: A/B test — persistent context vs isolated newContext in same browser

content:
[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 5. Investigation Journey — What We Tried > Attempt 11: A/B test — bundled Chromium vs system Chrome]

**Hypothesis:** playwright-cli might use a different Chrome binary than our scripts. Playwright bundles its own Chromium; `channel: 'chrome'` uses system-installed Chrome. Maybe there's a WASM or browser capability difference.
**Test:** Modified the debug script to run both: Test 1 with bundled Chromium (no `channel` option), Test 2 with `channel: 'chrome'`.
**Result:** ❌ BOTH FAILED. Bundled Chromium (v141) and system Chrome (v146) both show 4 error widgets.
**Conclusion:** The browser binary is not the differentiator.

---

[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 5. Investigation Journey — What We Tried > Attempt 12: Re-verify playwright-cli still works (sanity check)]

**Hypothesis:** Maybe the backend degraded further and playwright-cli no longer works either.
**Test:** Re-ran playwright-cli navigation to the dashboard after all the script tests.
**Result:** ✅ STILL WORKS. All 4 widgets loaded perfectly. Ticket Count Metric showed 1,506.
**Conclusion:** The backend is fine. The issue is definitively about playwright-cli vs all programmatic Playwright approaches.

---

metadata.section: Attempt 12: Re-verify playwright-cli still works (sanity check)
headingHierarchy: Attempt 12: Re-verify playwright-cli still works (sanity check)

content:
[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 5. Investigation Journey — What We Tried > The Breakthrough — Automation Detection Hypothesis]

After exhausting all context/browser/caching hypotheses, we focused on the **one remaining difference**: how playwright-cli identifies itself to the browser vs how every Playwright API call does.

The key realization:

> **Playwright Test always sets `navigator.webdriver = true`** via its CDP (Chrome DevTools Protocol) connection. This is the W3C WebDriver standard property that all browser automation tools set. playwright-cli does NOT set this — it connects as a developer tool, not a WebDriver client.

This means any JavaScript code on the page can detect whether it's running in an automated context by checking:
```javascript
if (navigator.webdriver === true) {
  // Automation detected
}
```

The DevRev frontend appears to have introduced this check as part of a deployment, and when automation is detected, it **skips the DuckDB-WASM initialization** and routes directly to `oasis.data.query`. Since `oasis.data.query` is broken (500) in preprod-jp, all widgets crash.

This hypothesis perfectly explains **all observations**:
- playwright-cli works → `navigator.webdriver` is `undefined` → DuckDB initializes → widgets load
- All Playwright scripts fail → `navigator.webdriver` is `true` → DuckDB skipped → oasis.data.query 500 → widgets crash
- QA environment works → oasis.data.query probably returns 200 in QA (or DuckDB works there too)
- Manual browsing works → `navigator.webdriver` is `undefined` in normal browsers

---

metadata.section: Part 2: `src/fixtures/page.fixtures.ts` — Mask `navigator.webdriver` before page scripts run
headingHierarchy: Part 2: `src/fixtures/page.fixtures.ts` — Mask `navigator.webdriver` before page scripts run

content:
[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 6. The Solution > Why This Fix Is Appropriate]

One might ask: "Are we just hiding from the app that we're using automation?" The answer is nuanced:

**We're testing the real user code path.** The DevRev dashboard's primary rendering path is DuckDB-WASM — it's what real users always experience. By bypassing the automation detection, we ensure our tests exercise the same code path that real users hit, making the tests more meaningful, not less.

The `oasis.data.query` fallback path is a degraded fallback for environments where DuckDB can't run (e.g., very old browsers, WASM-disabled environments). Testing the broken fallback would test infrastructure problems, not product behavior.

playwright-cli — the standard DevRev developer tool — already bypasses automation detection by design. Our fix brings Playwright Test to parity with that behavior.

[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 6. The Solution > Verification]

After applying the fix, all 16 tests in `ticketsDashboard.test.ts` passed against preprod-jp with no retries:

```
16 passed (2.8m)
```

The DuckDB View Query console logs reappeared, confirming DuckDB-WASM initialized correctly:
```
[INFO] View Query: CREATE or REPLACE VIEW devrev.dim_ticket as SELECT * from read_parquet([...])
[INFO] View Query: CREATE or REPLACE VIEW devrev.dim_revo as SELECT * from read_parquet([...])
[INFO] View Query: CREATE or REPLACE VIEW devrev.dim_sla_tracker as SELECT * from read_parquet([...])
```

---

[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 7. Remaining Issue (Backend — Not Our Fix)]

`oasis.data.query` still returns HTTP 500 in preprod-jp. This is a **separate backend bug** that should be reported to the DevRev platform/backend team.

**Why it matters even with our fix in place:** Real users in environments where DuckDB-WASM cannot run (e.g., enterprise browsers with WASM execution policies disabled, browsers with very limited memory) will hit this fallback and see broken dashboard widgets. It is not visible in normal usage but represents a broken safety net.

**Suggested ticket:**
- **Title:** `oasis.data.query returns HTTP 500 in preprod-jp`
- **Reproduction:** Navigate to any dashboard in preprod-jp; open DevTools → Network → filter by `oasis.data.query`; all requests return 500
- **Impact:** Dashboard widgets crash for users whose browser cannot run DuckDB-WASM
- **Environment:** preprod-jp (QA is unaffected)

---

[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 8. Files Changed]

| File | Change | Reason |
|------|--------|--------|
| `playwright.config.ts` | Added `--disable-blink-features=AutomationControlled` to both project `launchOptions.args` | Suppresses Chrome's automation advertisement at the browser-process level |
| `src/fixtures/page.fixtures.ts` | Added `page.addInitScript()` to override `navigator.webdriver` | Masks the automation property at the JavaScript level before any page scripts run |
| `tests/platform/dashboards/ticketsDashboard.test.ts` | Removed `test.describe.only` | Was added during debugging to isolate the failing suite; not meant for production |

[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 8. Files Changed > Debug Artifacts Deleted]

These files were created during the investigation and deleted before committing:
- `debug-context.ts` — standalone diagnostic script for context/browser comparison
- `src/fixtures/browser.fixtures.ts` — attempted fixture override (approach didn't fix the issue)
- `.duckdb-cache/` — locally downloaded DuckDB WASM extension files for cache testing
- `debug-trace.zip` — trace file from a debugging session

---

[Dashboard Widget Failure Investigation > 9. Key Takeaways]

1. **`navigator.webdriver` is a double-edged sword.** It's the standard way for websites to detect automation, and apps can (and do) change behavior based on it. If tests suddenly start failing in a specific environment without any code changes, automation detection is a prime suspect.

2. **Two independent bugs can create a single confusing failure.** The DuckDB bypass and the `oasis.data.query` 500 error are unrelated, but their combination made the issue look like a single mysterious problem.

3. **playwright-cli vs Playwright Test behave differently at the CDP level.** This is rarely documented but important: playwright-cli does not set automation markers, making it behave more like a real user. If something works in playwright-cli but not in tests, automation detection is likely involved.

4. **Systematic isolation is the right approach for unexplained regressions.** We tested environment, auth, cache, browser binary, context type, and browser launch method before arriving at the correct hypothesis. Each eliminated variable narrowed the search space.

5. **The fix is two-layered because the detection is two-layered.** Chrome sets automation state at both the process level (Blink feature flags) and the JS level (`navigator.webdriver`). Both layers need to be addressed.

[🧪 Playwright SDET Interview Guide (Mid-Level) > 🎯 Objective]

Evaluate candidate’s ability to:

- Write maintainable Playwright tests
- Design scalable automation
- Handle real-world tradeoffs (performance vs isolation)
- Understand Playwright internals (fixtures, parallelism)

---

[🧪 Playwright SDET Interview Guide (Mid-Level) > 🧱 Interview Structure (60–75 mins)]

1. Introduction (5–10 mins)
2. Core Playwright Knowledge (15–20 mins)
3. Practical Code Review (15–25 mins)
4. Test Design & Tradeoffs (10–15 mins)
5. Behavioral (5–10 mins)

---

[🧪 Playwright SDET Interview Guide (Mid-Level) > 🧪 Section 1: Playwright Experience & Test Architecture > Questions]

- Walk me through a recent Playwright framework you've worked on or built. How was it structured?
- How do you organize your Page Object Models (POM), reusable components, and test files?
- How do you share setup and state across multiple tests (e.g., handling user authentication)?
- How do you manage test data and handle multiple testing environments?
- What is your approach to ensuring test reliability and minimizing flakiness when running in CI?

[🧪 Playwright SDET Interview Guide (Mid-Level) > 🧪 Section 1: Playwright Experience & Test Architecture > Expected Signals]

**✅ Understands:**

- Separation of concerns (Pages, Components, Tests)
- Modern Playwright features for state management (e.g., `storageState`, fixtures, global setup)
- Designing independent tests that can run safely in parallel
- Building maintainable, scalable automation architectures

**🚨 Red Flags:**

- Putting all logic into massive, unstructured test files
- Hardcoding test data, URLs, or environment variables
- No clear strategy for setup/teardown (e.g., duplicating login steps in every test)

[🧪 Playwright SDET Interview Guide (Mid-Level) > 🧪 Section 1: Playwright Experience & Test Architecture > 📝 Evaluation]

[🧪 Playwright SDET Interview Guide (Mid-Level) > ⚖️ Section 4: Test Dependency & Tradeoffs > Scenario]

👉 “You have:

- Test A creates a user (10–15 min UI flow)
- Test B needs that user

Do you:

1. Duplicate the flow?
2. Depend on Test A?”

[🧪 Playwright SDET Interview Guide (Mid-Level) > ⚖️ Section 4: Test Dependency & Tradeoffs > Ideal Answer]

> “Tests should be independent by default. Instead of duplicating UI setup or creating dependency, I would extract setup using API, fixtures, or saved state.”

[🧪 Playwright SDET Interview Guide (Mid-Level) > ⚖️ Section 4: Test Dependency & Tradeoffs > Decision Framework]

- **✅ Best Option (Avoid UI Setup):** Use API, seed data, or backend setup.
- **✅ Better Option (Reuse State):** Use fixtures or `storageState`.
- **✅ Acceptable (Serial Execution):** `test.describe.serial()` when testing a full workflow or setup is inherently sequential.
- **❌ Avoid:** Test B directly relying on Test A result, hidden dependencies.

[🧪 Playwright SDET Interview Guide (Mid-Level) > ⚖️ Section 4: Test Dependency & Tradeoffs > Follow-Up Questions]

- 👉 “What if API is not available?”
  - _Expected:_ Use fixtures, reuse browser state, or serialize with justification.
- 👉 “What happens in parallel execution if tests depend on each other?”
  - _Expected:_ Race conditions, flaky failures.

- How do you debug failures in CI?
- Have you ever encountered a situation where Playwright "is too fast" (e.g., it clicks a button, but nothing happens)? How did you solve it?

[🧪 Playwright SDET Interview Guide (Mid-Level) > 🧪 Section 5: Debugging & Reliability > Expected Answers]

- Use Playwright trace viewer
- Check timing issues and race conditions
- Avoid hard waits (`waitForTimeout`)
- Inspect network calls and console logs
- Understands hydration issues in modern SPAs (React, Next.js). They know that the UI might render before event listeners are attached. Good solutions include: waiting for a specific network request to complete, waiting for a specific element state, or using auto-retrying assertions before interacting.

[Investigation: reCAPTCHA Enterprise Blocks Automated Org Deletion]

**Date:** 2026-03-30
**Test:** `tests/computer/computerOnboarding.test.ts` — `afterAll` cleanup hook
**Environment:** prod-sg (`app.devrev.ai`)
**Status:** Unresolved — workaround needed

---

metadata.section: Investigation: reCAPTCHA Enterprise Blocks Automated Org Deletion
headingHierarchy: Investigation: reCAPTCHA Enterprise Blocks Automated Org Deletion

content:
[Investigation: reCAPTCHA Enterprise Blocks Automated Org Deletion > Problem Statement]

The `computerOnboarding.test.ts` suite creates temporary DevOrgs during test execution and attempts to delete them in `afterAll` hooks. The deletion consistently fails with **"Access denied — You do not have the necessary permissions to perform this action"**, leaving orphaned test orgs behind.

The same deletion flow works when performed through a manually authenticated browser session (playwright-cli with human authentication).

---

headingHierarchy: Investigation: reCAPTCHA Enterprise Blocks Automated Org Deletion

content:
[Investigation: reCAPTCHA Enterprise Blocks Automated Org Deletion > Root Cause Recap]

reCAPTCHA Enterprise session scoring blocks automated org deletion. The dev-orgs.delete API requires an
x-devrev-captcha header token, and Google scores the entire session's behavior — mouse movements, keyboard patterns,
timing, etc.

- playwright-cli works — manual authentication creates human-like signals, giving a high trust score that carries
  through the session
- Playwright automation fails — a fresh context with fully automated OTP login + immediate delete yields a low trust
  score → "Access denied"

Everything we tried failed: masking navigator.webdriver, programmatic fetch() with captured headers, programmatic
grecaptcha.enterprise.execute() — all produce low-score tokens.

---

headingHierarchy: Investigation: reCAPTCHA Enterprise Blocks Automated Org Deletion
Investigation: reCAPTCHA Enterprise Blocks Automated Org Deletion

Exported the HAR file and analyzed the successful `dev-orgs.delete` request:

**Request headers (relevant):**

| `x-devrev-captcha`              | `0cAFcWeA69M52Z3Q...` (reCAPTCHA Enterprise token, ~200 chars) |
| `x-devrev-client-id`            | `ai.devrev.web-product.prod`                                   |

| `x-devrev-client-version`       | `a683a98`                                                      |
| `x-devrev-dev-user-don`         | `don:identity:dvrv-sg-1:devo/814RlJzkfz:devu/1`                |
| `x-devrev-session-id`           | `b0a356d8-c5cf-4699-b464-6a046c408564`                         |
| `x-devrev-include-oasis-format` | `true`                                                         |

**Request body:**

```json
{ "dev_slug": "autotestcomputerandappsorg1774881978727" }
```

**Response:** `200 OK` (empty body)

**Key finding:** The `x-devrev-captcha` header contains a **reCAPTCHA Enterprise token** (site key: `6Lemni8mAAAAAJ3aTXH554RnEkbyMpi-iholX1Nw`). This token is generated client-side by the app's JavaScript via `grecaptcha.enterprise.execute()` before the delete API call is made.

content:

Clicked "Delete permanently" via the UI in the same browser session where the user manually authenticated.

metadata.section: Test C: UI click in the same playwright-cli session
headingHierarchy: Test C: UI click in the same playwright-cli session

content:
[=> Redirected to /login — SUCCESS > Root Cause > reCAPTCHA Enterprise session scoring]

The `dev-orgs.delete` endpoint is protected by **reCAPTCHA Enterprise** (invisible, score-based). The app calls `grecaptcha.enterprise.execute()` when the user clicks "Delete permanently" and sends the resulting token in the `x-devrev-captcha` request header. The server validates this token with Google's reCAPTCHA Enterprise API and receives a **session risk score** (0.0 = bot, 1.0 = human).

reCAPTCHA Enterprise evaluates the **entire session's behavior**, not just the individual action:

- Mouse movement patterns
- Keyboard interaction patterns
- Click timing and coordinates
- Session duration and navigation patterns
- Browser fingerprinting signals

[=> Redirected to /login — SUCCESS > Root Cause > Why it works in playwright-cli (manual auth)]

When the user manually authenticates (clicking buttons, typing credentials), reCAPTCHA Enterprise observes human-like behavior throughout the session. The subsequent programmatic delete (automated via playwright-cli) inherits this **high session trust score** because it happens in the same browser context.

metadata.section: Why it works in playwright-cli (manual auth)
headingHierarchy: Why it works in playwright-cli (manual auth)

content:
[=> Redirected to /login — SUCCESS > Root Cause > Why it fails in the test afterAll]

The `createCleanupPage` function creates a **brand-new browser context** with:

- Zero prior interaction history
- Fully automated OTP login (no human mouse/keyboard signals)
- Immediate navigation to settings and delete action

reCAPTCHA Enterprise assigns this session a **low trust score** because every signal indicates automation. The `x-devrev-captcha` token generated by `grecaptcha.enterprise.execute()` carries this low score, and the server rejects the delete action with "Access denied" (403 Forbidden).

metadata.section: Why it fails in the test afterAll
headingHierarchy: Why it fails in the test afterAll

content:
[=> Redirected to /login — SUCCESS > Root Cause > Additional issue: spotlight nudge handler]

A secondary issue was also discovered. The `dismissPromotionalBanners()` handler in `src/utils/page.utils.ts` fails to dismiss a spotlight nudge iframe that overlays the delete dialog. The handler's hover-then-click-first-button strategy is broken because internal iframe elements intercept the hover action. This causes the handler to retry 47 times and eventually timeout, blocking the entire `deleteOrg()` flow even before the reCAPTCHA issue is reached. This is a separate fix from the reCAPTCHA problem.

---

[=> Redirected to /login — SUCCESS > What we know doesn't work]

| Approach                                                                                    | Result           | Reason                                                              |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| New browser context + automated OTP login + UI delete                                       | Access denied    | reCAPTCHA low score for fully automated session                     |
| `fetch()` with cookies + `credentials: 'include'`                                           | 401 Unauthorized | Server requires custom `x-devrev-*` auth headers, not just cookies  |
| `fetch()` with captured auth headers + programmatic `grecaptcha.enterprise.execute()` token | 403 Forbidden    | reCAPTCHA token carries the session's low trust score               |
| Masking `navigator.webdriver` (already in place)                                            | Not sufficient   | reCAPTCHA Enterprise uses many signals beyond `navigator.webdriver` |
| `--disable-blink-features=AutomationControlled` (already in place)                          | Not sufficient   | Same as above                                                       |

---

[=> Redirected to /login — SUCCESS > Options for Resolution > Option 1: Backend feature toggle to bypass reCAPTCHA (Recommended)]

Ask the DevRev backend team to add a feature toggle or test-environment exemption that skips reCAPTCHA validation on the `dev-orgs.delete` endpoint.

**Pros:**

- Clean, permanent solution
- No test code workarounds needed
- Aligns with testing best practices (test environments shouldn't have bot protection)

**Cons:**

- Requires backend team involvement
- May need security review/approval

**Implementation:** Add a feature toggle key to `src/config/featureToggles.registry.ts` and use `setFeatureToggle()` utility to disable captcha before deletion. _Note: the Control Panel UI is currently inaccessible (since 2026-03-26), so this toggle would need to be set via API or backend configuration._

metadata.section: Option 1: Backend feature toggle to bypass reCAPTCHA (Recommended)
headingHierarchy: Option 1: Backend feature toggle to bypass reCAPTCHA (Recommended)

content:
[=> Redirected to /login — SUCCESS > Options for Resolution > Option 2: API token/service account for test cleanup]

Request a dedicated API token or service account that can call `dev-orgs.delete` without reCAPTCHA validation.

**Pros:**

- Decouples cleanup from browser automation
- Fast and reliable
- Can be used in CI/CD pipelines

**Cons:**

- Requires backend team to provision
- Needs secure token storage in CI

**Implementation:** Add an API utility in `src/utils/` that calls `dev-orgs.delete` with a service token, and use it in `afterAll` hooks.

metadata.section: Option 2: API token/service account for test cleanup
headingHierarchy: Option 2: API token/service account for test cleanup

metadata.section: Option 3: Reuse the test's existing page context for cleanup
headingHierarchy: Option 3: Reuse the test's existing page context for cleanup

metadata.section: Option 4: Best-effort cleanup with fallback logging
headingHierarchy: Option 4: Best-effort cleanup with fallback logging

content:
[=> Redirected to /login — SUCCESS > Options for Resolution > Option 5: Pre-test cleanup of stale orgs]

Instead of cleaning up after each test run, run a periodic cleanup job (manually or via CI) using playwright-cli with manual authentication to delete accumulated test orgs.

**Pros:**

- Separates test execution from cleanup concerns
- Uses proven manual-auth workflow

**Cons:**

- Requires manual intervention (human must authenticate)
- Stale orgs accumulate between cleanup runs
- Not fully automated

---

metadata.section: Option 5: Pre-test cleanup of stale orgs
headingHierarchy: Option 5: Pre-test cleanup of stale orgs

content:
[=> Redirected to /login — SUCCESS > Recommendation]

**Short term (now):** Implement **Option 4** (best-effort cleanup with logging) to unblock test development. Also fix the `dismissPromotionalBanners` handler (separate issue) to prevent the spotlight nudge from blocking the delete dialog.

**Medium term:** Pursue **Option 1** or **Option 2** with the DevRev backend team to get a proper reCAPTCHA bypass for test environments.

---

[=> Redirected to /login — SUCCESS > Related Files]

| File                                            | Relevance                                                |
| ----------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| `tests/computer/computerOnboarding.test.ts`     | Test file with failing afterAll hooks                    |
| `src/pages/settings/general.page.ts`            | `deleteOrg()` method                                     |
| `src/utils/page.utils.ts`                       | `dismissPromotionalBanners()` handler (secondary issue)  |
| `src/config/featureToggles.registry.ts`         | Feature toggle registry (for potential reCAPTCHA toggle) |
| `src/utils/controlPanelFeatureToggles.utils.ts` | `setFeatureToggle()` utility                             |
| `playwright.computer.config.ts`                 | Computer test Playwright configuration                   |
| `delete-org-playwright-cli.har`                 | HAR capture of successful delete (for reference)         |

[=> Redirected to /login — SUCCESS > Related Issues]

- DevOrg creation limit: 10 per user (orphaned test orgs consume this quota)
- Control Panel inaccessible since 2026-03-26 (limits feature toggle options)
- `dismissPromotionalBanners` handler broken by nudge iframe DOM changes (separate fix needed)

[CircleCI Configuration]

This directory contains the CircleCI configuration for the Sentinel automation framework.

[CircleCI Configuration > File Structure]

```
.circleci/
├── config.yml       # All jobs and workflows (~1100 lines)
└── README.md        # This file
```

[CircleCI Configuration > Architecture]

The configuration uses a single `config.yml` file containing all jobs and workflows for both Sentinel and Computer tests. This approach ensures reliable execution of scheduled workflows.

[CircleCI Configuration > Architecture > Why Single File?]

CircleCI's dynamic configuration (`setup: true`) has a limitation: scheduled workflows defined in the setup config are ignored. Since we need scheduled workflows for both Sentinel and Computer tests, we use a single config file without dynamic configuration.

[CircleCI Configuration > Architecture > Organization]

The config is organized into clear sections:

1. **Parameters** - Pipeline parameters for triggering different test types
2. **Slack Anchors** - Notification templates for each trigger type
3. **Jobs** - Test execution definitions (lint, test, smoke-test, regression-test, computer-tests)
4. **Workflows** - Scheduled, manual, and post-deployment triggers

[CircleCI Configuration > Slack Notifications]

All Slack notifications include a **Trigger** field to identify how the test was initiated:

| Trigger Type | Indicator | When Used |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| Scheduled | ⏰ Scheduled | Cron-triggered workflows |
| Manual | 👤 Manual | UI/API-triggered workflows |
| Post-Deployment | 🚀 Post-Deployment | Deployment-triggered workflows |

[CircleCI Configuration > Slack Notifications > Example Notification]

```
✅ Sentinel Smoke Tests Passed

Trigger: ⏰ Scheduled
Environment: prod-sg
App Version: 174c8ca

View Build: https://circleci.com/...
View Report: https://output.circle-artifacts.com/...
```

[CircleCI Configuration > Slack Notifications > Notification Channels]

| Test Type | QA Channel | Prod SG / Prod Channel | Variable |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------|----------|
| Sentinel Smoke | `sentinel-ui-test-runs` | `sentinel-ui-production-runs` | `SLACK_DEFAULT_CHANNEL` |
| Sentinel Regression | `sentinel-ui-regression-runs` | `sentinel-ui-production-runs` | `SLACK_REGRESSION_CHANNEL` |
| Computer | `sentinel-ui-computer-runs` | `sentinel-ui-production-runs` | `SLACK_COMPUTER_CHANNEL` |

> **Note:** Prod and Prod SG use a dedicated channel (`sentinel-ui-production-runs`) for all test results (smoke, regression, computer). The channel is controlled per-environment via the CircleCI context variables.

[CircleCI Configuration > Environment Variables Setup > Project Environment Variables (Non-sensitive)]

*Settings → Environment Variables*

Only non-sensitive values should live at the project level. Currently:

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `LOGIN_EMAIL` | Auth0 login email used across environments |

[CircleCI Configuration > Environment Variables Setup > CircleCI Contexts (Sensitive + Environment-Specific)]

Both Sentinel and Computer tests use the same contexts:

- `sentinel-qa-environment`
- `sentinel-prod-sg-environment`
- `sentinel-preprod-jp-environment` (self-contained, all JP credentials + config)
- `sentinel-prod-environment`

[CircleCI Configuration > Environment Variables Setup > Context Variables for `sentinel-prod-sg-environment`]

| Variable | Description | Type |
|----------|-------------|------|
| `TEST_ENV` | Environment name (`prod-sg`) | Env-specific |
| `BASE_URL` | Application base URL | Env-specific |
| `COMPUTER_BASE_URL` | Computer app base URL | Env-specific |
| `COMPUTER_ONLY_ORG_SLUG` | Org slug for computer-only org | Env-specific |
| `COMPUTER_APPS_ORG_SLUG` | Org slug for computer-and-apps org | Env-specific |
| `DEVREV_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN` | Service account token | Env-specific |
| `SLACK_ACCESS_TOKEN` | Slack bot token | Shared |
| `SLACK_DEFAULT_CHANNEL` | Channel for Sentinel smoke tests (`sentinel-ui-production-runs`) | Env-specific |
| `SLACK_REGRESSION_CHANNEL` | Channel for Sentinel regression tests (`sentinel-ui-production-runs`) | Env-specific |
| `SLACK_COMPUTER_CHANNEL` | Channel for Computer tests (`sentinel-ui-production-runs`) | Env-specific |
| `GMAIL_BILLING_ADMIN_APP_PASSWORD` | Gmail app password | `qa.testuser.devrev@gmail.com` |
| `GMAIL_ADMIN_APP_PASSWORD` | Gmail app password | `auto-user1@devrev.ai` |
| `GMAIL_MEMBER_APP_PASSWORD` | Gmail app password | `auto-user2@devrev.ai` |
| `LOGIN_PASSWORD` | Auth0 login password | Sensitive |

[CircleCI Configuration > Environment Variables Setup > Context Variables for `sentinel-preprod-jp-environment`]

This context is **self-contained** — it includes all credentials and JP-specific config. It does not depend on any other context.

| Variable | Description | Value |
|----------|-------------|-------|
| `TEST_ENV` | Environment name | `preprod-jp` |
| `BASE_URL` | Application base URL | `https://app.devrev.ai` |
| `COMPUTER_BASE_URL` | Computer app base URL | `https://app.devrev.ai` |
| `ORG_SLUG` | Organization slug | `preprod-ne1-automation-support` |
| `COMPUTER_ONLY_ORG_SLUG` | Org slug for computer-only org | `preprod-jp-automation-computer-only` |
| `COMPUTER_APPS_ORG_SLUG` | Org slug for computer-and-apps org | `preprod-jp-automation-computer-and-apps` |
| `OTP_FROM_CONTAINS` | OTP email sender filter | `otp@devrev.ai` |
| `DEVREV_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN` | Service account token | Sensitive |
| `SLACK_ACCESS_TOKEN` | Slack bot token | Sensitive |
| `SLACK_DEFAULT_CHANNEL` | Channel for smoke tests | `sentinel-ui-test-runs` |
| `SLACK_REGRESSION_CHANNEL` | Channel for regression tests | `sentinel-ui-regression-runs` |
| `SLACK_COMPUTER_CHANNEL` | Channel for computer tests | `sentinel-ui-computer-runs` |
| `GMAIL_BILLING_ADMIN_APP_PASSWORD` | Gmail app password | Sensitive |
| `GMAIL_ADMIN_APP_PASSWORD` | Gmail app password | Sensitive |
| `GMAIL_MEMBER_APP_PASSWORD` | Gmail app password | Sensitive |
| `LOGIN_PASSWORD` | Auth0 login password | Sensitive |

[CircleCI Configuration > Environment Variables Setup > How to Get Gmail App Passwords]

1. Sign in to the Google Account for each test email
2. Go to Google Account → Security → 2-Step Verification
3. At the bottom, click "App passwords"
4. Select app: "Mail", Select device: "Other (Custom name)"
5. Enter a name like "CircleCI Sentinel"
6. Copy the 16-character password (no spaces)

[CircleCI Configuration > Triggering Tests > Manual Trigger via UI]

1. Go to CircleCI → Project → Trigger Pipeline
2. Set parameters:
   - For Sentinel smoke: `run_manual_smoke_test: true`, `test_env: <env>`
   - For Sentinel regression: `run_manual_regression_test: true`, `test_env: <env>`
   - For Computer: `run_manual_computer_test: true`, `test_env: <env>`

content:
[Trigger Computer post-deployment (QA example) > Schedule Summary > Sentinel Smoke Tests]

| Environment | Times (IST) | Cron (UTC) |
|-------------|-------------|------------|
| QA | 07:00, 19:00 | `30 1`, `30 13` |
| Prod SG | 07:00, 14:00 | `30 1`, `30 8` |
| Prod | 19:00, 23:00, hourly | `30 13`, `30 17`, `0 *` |

[Trigger Computer post-deployment (QA example) > Schedule Summary > Sentinel Regression Tests]

| Environment | Times (IST) | Cron (UTC) |
|-------------|-------------|------------|
| QA | 03:00, 15:00 | `30 21`, `30 9` |
| Prod SG | 05:00, 17:00 | `30 23`, `30 11` |
| Prod | 08:00, 20:00 | `30 2`, `30 14` |

[Trigger Computer post-deployment (QA example) > Troubleshooting > Tests Don't Run on Schedule]

- Verify cron syntax is correct (CircleCI uses UTC)
- Ensure branch filter is set to `main`
- Check that the workflow doesn't have conflicting `when` conditions

[Trigger Computer post-deployment (QA example) > Troubleshooting > Manual Trigger Doesn't Work]

- Verify you're setting the correct parameter (`run_manual_computer_test` vs `run_manual_smoke_test`)
- Ensure `test_env` parameter matches the workflow condition

[Trigger Computer post-deployment (QA example) > Troubleshooting > Context Variables Not Available]

- Ensure the context is attached to the job in the workflow
- Verify variable names match exactly (case-sensitive)
- Check context permissions in CircleCI

[Trigger Computer post-deployment (QA example) > Troubleshooting > Slack Notifications Not Sent]

- Verify `SLACK_ACCESS_TOKEN` is set in the context
- Verify channel variables are set (`SLACK_DEFAULT_CHANNEL`, `SLACK_REGRESSION_CHANNEL`, `SLACK_COMPUTER_CHANNEL`)
- Ensure the Slack bot is added to the target channels

[Trigger Computer post-deployment (QA example) > Future: Adding Prod (US) Computer Tests]

Prod SG already runs Computer tests. When ready to add Computer tests for Prod (US):

1. Add Computer-specific variables to existing contexts:
   - Add `COMPUTER_BASE_URL` to `sentinel-prod-environment`
   - Add `COMPUTER_ONLY_ORG_SLUG` to `sentinel-prod-environment`
   - Add `COMPUTER_APPS_ORG_SLUG` to `sentinel-prod-environment`
   - Add `SLACK_COMPUTER_CHANNEL` if not already present

2. Add scheduled workflows to `config.yml`:
   ```yaml
   prod-computer-tests-XXXX:
     triggers:
       - schedule:
           cron: '...'
     jobs:
       - computer-tests:
           context: sentinel-prod-environment
   ```

3. Add manual and post-deployment workflows with new parameters

version: 2.1

########################################
# ORBS
########################################

orbs:
  slack: circleci/slack@5.1.1

########################################
# PIPELINE PARAMETERS
########################################

parameters:
  # Environment selection
  test_env:
    type: enum
    enum: ['qa', 'prod-sg', 'preprod-jp', 'prod']
    default: 'qa'

  # Sentinel parameters
  run_manual_smoke_test:
    type: boolean
    default: false
  run_post_deployment_smoke_test:
    type: boolean
    default: false
  run_manual_regression_test:
    type: boolean
    default: false

  # Computer parameters
  run_manual_computer_test:
    type: boolean
    default: false
  run_post_deployment_computer_test:
    type: boolean
    default: false

########################################
# YAML ANCHORS - SENTINEL (SCHEDULED)
########################################

slack-notification-pass: &slack-notification-pass
  slack/notify:
    event: pass
    custom: |
      {
        "text": "✅ *Sentinel Smoke Tests Passed*\n\n*Trigger:* ⏰ Scheduled\n*Environment:* ${TEST_ENV}\n*Region:* ${REGION}\n*App Version:* ${APP_VERSION}\n*Plug Version:* ${PLUG_VERSION}\n\nView Build: $CIRCLE_BUILD_URL\nView Report: https://output.circle-artifacts.com/output/job/$CIRCLE_WORKFLOW_JOB_ID/artifacts/0/playwright-report/index.html"
      }
    channel: ${SLACK_DEFAULT_CHANNEL}

slack-notification-fail: &slack-notification-fail
  slack/notify:
    event: fail
    custom: |
      {
        "text": "❌ *Sentinel Smoke Tests Failed*\n\n*Trigger:* ⏰ Scheduled\n*Environment:* ${TEST_ENV}\n*Region:* ${REGION}\n*App Version:* ${APP_VERSION}\n*Plug Version:* ${PLUG_VERSION}\n\nView Build: $CIRCLE_BUILD_URL\nView Report: https://output.circle-artifacts.com/output/job/$CIRCLE_WORKFLOW_JOB_ID/artifacts/0/playwright-report/index.html"
      }
    channel: ${SLACK_DEFAULT_CHANNEL}

slack-regression-notification-pass: &slack-regression-notification-pass
  slack/notify:
    event: pass
    custom: |
      {
        "text": "✅ *Sentinel Regression Tests Passed*\n\n*Trigger:* ⏰ Scheduled\n*Environment:* ${TEST_ENV}\n*Region:* ${REGION}\n*App Version:* ${APP_VERSION}\n*Plug Version:* ${PLUG_VERSION}\n\nView Build: $CIRCLE_BUILD_URL\nView Report: https://output.circle-artifacts.com/output/job/$CIRCLE_WORKFLOW_JOB_ID/artifacts/0/playwright-report/index.html"
      }
    channel: ${SLACK_REGRESSION_CHANNEL}

slack-regression-notification-fail: &slack-regression-notification-fail
  slack/notify:
    event: fail
    custom: |
      {
        "text": "❌ *Sentinel Regression Tests Failed*\n\n*Trigger:* ⏰ Scheduled\n*Environment:* ${TEST_ENV}\n*Region:* ${REGION}\n*App Version:* ${APP_VERSION}\n*Plug Version:* ${PLUG_VERSION}\n\nView Build: $CIRCLE_BUILD_URL\nView Report: https://output.circle-artifacts.com/output/job/$CIRCLE_WORKFLOW_JOB_ID/artifacts/0/playwright-report/index.html"
      }
    channel: ${SLACK_REGRESSION_CHANNEL}

########################################
# YAML ANCHORS - COMPUTER (SCHEDULED)
########################################

slack-computer-notification-pass: &slack-computer-notification-pass
  slack/notify:
    event: pass
    custom: |
      {

########################################
# JOBS - CI/CD
########################################

jobs:
  lint:
    docker:
      - image: mcr.microsoft.com/playwright:v1.56.0-noble
    working_directory: ~/project
    steps:
      - checkout

      - restore_cache:
          keys:
            - v1-npm-{{ checksum "package-lock.json" }}
            - v1-npm-

      - run:
          name: Install dependencies
          command: npm ci

      - save_cache:
          key: v1-npm-{{ checksum "package-lock.json" }}
          paths:
            - ~/.npm
            - node_modules

      - run:
          name: Run lint checks
          command: npm run lint:all

  test:
    docker:
      - image: mcr.microsoft.com/playwright:v1.56.0-noble
    resource_class: large
    working_directory: ~/project
    environment:
      TEST_ENV: << pipeline.parameters.test_env >>
      PLAYWRIGHT_SKIP_BROWSER_DOWNLOAD: '1'
    steps:
      - checkout

      - restore_cache:
          keys:
            - v1-npm-{{ checksum "package-lock.json" }}
            - v1-npm-

      - run:
          name: Install dependencies
          command: npm ci

      - save_cache:
          key: v1-npm-{{ checksum "package-lock.json" }}
          paths:
            - ~/.npm
            - node_modules

      - run:
          name: Run Playwright tests
          no_output_timeout: 40m
          command: |
            if [ "$CIRCLE_BRANCH" = "develop" ] || [ "$CIRCLE_BRANCH" = "main" ]; then
              # Full test suite on develop/main
              echo "Running full test suite on ${CIRCLE_BRANCH}"
              npx playwright test
            else
              # Changed tests only for PRs and feature branches
              echo "Running tests for branch: ${CIRCLE_BRANCH}"
              git fetch -q origin develop || true
              
              # Check if any test files changed
              CHANGED_TEST_FILES=$(git diff --name-only origin/develop...HEAD | grep -E '\.(spec|test)\.(js|ts|jsx|tsx)$' || true)
              
              if [ -n "$CHANGED_TEST_FILES" ]; then
                echo "Test files changed, running only changed tests:"
                echo "$CHANGED_TEST_FILES"
                npx playwright test --only-changed=origin/develop
              else
                echo "No test files changed, skipping tests (will run on merge to develop)"
                exit 0
              fi
            fi

      - store_artifacts:
          path: playwright-report
          when: always

      - store_artifacts:
          path: test-results
          when: always

      - store_test_results:
          path: test-results

  ########################################
  # JOBS - SENTINEL AUTOMATION
  ########################################

  smoke-test:
    docker:
      - image: mcr.microsoft.com/playwright:v1.56.0-noble
    resource_class: large
    working_directory: ~/project
    environment:
      PLAYWRIGHT_SKIP_BROWSER_DOWNLOAD: '1'
    steps:
      - checkout

      - restore_cache:
          keys:
            - v1-npm-{{ checksum "package-lock.json" }}
            - v1-npm-

      - run:
          name: Install dependencies
          command: npm ci

      - save_cache:
          key: v1-npm-{{ checksum "package-lock.json" }}
          paths:
            - ~/.npm
            - node_modules

      - run:
          name: Run Smoke Tests
          no_output_timeout: 40m
          command: |
            echo "Running smoke tests for environment: ${TEST_ENV}"
            rm -rf blob-report playwright-report
            mkdir -p blob-report/batch1 blob-report/batch2 blob-report/all

            BATCH1_EXIT=0
            BATCH2_EXIT=0

            # Baseline smoke tests run in parallel and exclude shared-state feature-toggle suites.
            # Write blob output to a dedicated folder so the next Playwright invocation does not clear it.
            PLAYWRIGHT_BLOB_OUTPUT_DIR=blob-report/batch1 \
            npx playwright test --grep @smoke --grep-invert @feature-toggle --pass-with-no-tests --reporter=line,blob || BATCH1_EXIT=$?

            # Feature-toggle smoke tests mutate shared workspace state, so run them in a separate serialized batch.
            # This batch also needs its own blob folder so both runs can be merged afterward.
            # The grep regex selects only tests tagged with both @smoke and @feature-toggle.
            PLAYWRIGHT_BLOB_OUTPUT_DIR=blob-report/batch2 \
            npx playwright test --grep "(?=.*@smoke)(?=.*@feature-toggle)" --workers=1 --pass-with-no-tests --reporter=line,blob || BATCH2_EXIT=$?

            cp blob-report/batch1/*.zip blob-report/all/ 2>/dev/null || true
            cp blob-report/batch2/*.zip blob-report/all/ 2>/dev/null || true

            PLAYWRIGHT_HTML_OUTPUT_DIR=playwright-report \
            PLAYWRIGHT_JSON_OUTPUT_NAME=playwright-report/results.json \
            PLAYWRIGHT_JUNIT_OUTPUT_NAME=playwright-report/results.xml \
            npx playwright merge-reports --reporter=html,json,junit ./blob-report/all

            if [ "$BATCH1_EXIT" -ne 0 ] || [ "$BATCH2_EXIT" -ne 0 ]; then
              exit 1
            fi

      - run:
          name: Export APP_VERSION, PLUG_VERSION and REGION for notifications
          when: always
          command: |
            if [ -f "app-version.txt" ]; then
              APP_VERSION=$(cat app-version.txt)
              echo "export APP_VERSION='${APP_VERSION}'" >> "$BASH_ENV"
              echo "APP_VERSION: ${APP_VERSION}"
            else
              echo "export APP_VERSION='unknown'" >> "$BASH_ENV"
              echo "APP_VERSION file not found, using 'unknown'"
            fi

            if [ -f "plug-version.txt" ]; then
              PLUG_VERSION=$(cat plug-version.txt)
              echo "export PLUG_VERSION='${PLUG_VERSION}'" >> "$BASH_ENV"
              echo "PLUG_VERSION: ${PLUG_VERSION}"
            else
              echo "export PLUG_VERSION='unknown'" >> "$BASH_ENV"
              echo "PLUG_VERSION file not found, using 'unknown'"
            fi

            case "${TEST_ENV}" in
              qa) REGION="US" ;;
              prod-sg) REGION="Singapore" ;;
              preprod-jp) REGION="Japan" ;;
              prod) REGION="US" ;;
              *) REGION="Unknown" ;;
            esac
            echo "export REGION='${REGION}'" >> "$BASH_ENV"
            echo "REGION: ${REGION}"

      - store_artifacts:
          path: playwright-report
          when: always

      - store_artifacts:
          path: test-results
          when: always

      - store_test_results:
          path: test-results

      # OASIS Integration: Convert JSON to Parquet and upload to OASIS
      - run:
          name: Convert report to Parquet and upload to OASIS
          when: always
          command: |
            if [ -f "playwright-report/results.json" ]; then
              # Validate TEST_ENV is set (provided via CircleCI context)
              if [ -z "${TEST_ENV}" ]; then
                echo "Warning: TEST_ENV not set, skipping OASIS upload"
                exit 0
              fi
              
              echo "Installing Python dependencies for OASIS upload..."
              # Playwright Docker image doesn't have pip - install it first
              apt-get update -qq && apt-get install -y -qq python3-pip >/dev/null 2>&1 || {
                echo "Warning: Failed to install pip, skipping OASIS upload"
                exit 0
              }
              
              # Install required packages
              python3 -m pip install --user --quiet --break-system-packages pandas pyarrow requests || {
                echo "Warning: Failed to install Python packages, skipping OASIS upload"
                exit 0
              }
              
              # All test results go to dev0 (Prod DevRev org)
              DEVREV_API_ENDPOINT="https://api.devrev.ai"
              echo "Using API endpoint: ${DEVREV_API_ENDPOINT} (dev0)"
              
              # Generate master timestamp for this execution
              MASTER_TIMESTAMP=$(date -u +"%Y%m%d_%H%M%S")
              echo "Master timestamp: ${MASTER_TIMESTAMP}"
              
              # Read region from file (set by login.setup.ts during test execution)
              REGION=$(cat region.txt 2>/dev/null || echo "unknown")
              echo "Detected region: ${REGION}"
              
              # Step 1: Convert Playwright JSON to Parquet
              echo "Converting Playwright report to Parquet format..."
              python3 scripts/oasis/convert_to_parquet.py \
                --report ./playwright-report/results.json \
                --output ./playwright-report/report.parquet \
                --environment "${TEST_ENV}" \
                --region "${REGION}" \
                --service "sentinel" \
                --version "${APP_VERSION:-unknown}" \
                --timestamp "${MASTER_TIMESTAMP}" \
                --test-type "smoke" \
                || {
                  echo "Warning: Parquet conversion failed, skipping OASIS upload"
                  exit 0
                }
              
              echo "Parquet file created successfully"
              
              # Step 2: Upload to OASIS (requires service account token)
              if [ -n "${DEVREV_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN}" ]; then
                echo "Uploading to OASIS..."
                python3 scripts/oasis/upload_to_oasis.py \
                  --parquet ./playwright-report/report.parquet \
                  --dataset "sentinel_playwright_test_results" \
                  --dataset-title "Sentinel Playwright Test Results" \
                  --dataset-description "Automated E2E test results from Sentinel Playwright tests" \
                  --api-endpoint "${DEVREV_API_ENDPOINT}" \
                  || echo "Warning: OASIS upload failed, continuing..."
              else
                echo "DEVREV_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN not set, skipping OASIS upload"
                echo "Parquet file is still available in artifacts"
              fi
            else
              echo "No Playwright JSON report found, skipping OASIS integration"
            fi

  regression-test:
    docker:
      - image: mcr.microsoft.com/playwright:v1.56.0-noble
    resource_class: large
    working_directory: ~/project
    environment:
      PLAYWRIGHT_SKIP_BROWSER_DOWNLOAD: '1'
    steps:
      - checkout

      - restore_cache:
          keys:
            - v1-npm-{{ checksum "package-lock.json" }}
            - v1-npm-

      - run:
          name: Install dependencies
          command: npm ci

      - save_cache:
          key: v1-npm-{{ checksum "package-lock.json" }}
          paths:
            - ~/.npm

      - run:
          name: Run Regression Tests
          no_output_timeout: 40m
          command: |
            echo "Running regression tests for environment: ${TEST_ENV}"
            npx playwright test --grep @regression

      - run:
          name: Export APP_VERSION, PLUG_VERSION and REGION for notifications
          when: always
          command: |
            if [ -f "app-version.txt" ]; then
              APP_VERSION=$(cat app-version.txt)
              echo "export APP_VERSION='${APP_VERSION}'" >> "$BASH_ENV"
              echo "APP_VERSION: ${APP_VERSION}"
            else
              echo "export APP_VERSION='unknown'" >> "$BASH_ENV"
              echo "APP_VERSION file not found, using 'unknown'"
            fi

            if [ -f "plug-version.txt" ]; then
              PLUG_VERSION=$(cat plug-version.txt)
              echo "export PLUG_VERSION='${PLUG_VERSION}'" >> "$BASH_ENV"
              echo "PLUG_VERSION: ${PLUG_VERSION}"
            else
              echo "export PLUG_VERSION='unknown'" >> "$BASH_ENV"
              echo "PLUG_VERSION file not found, using 'unknown'"
            fi

            case "${TEST_ENV}" in
              qa) REGION="US" ;;
              prod-sg) REGION="Singapore" ;;
              preprod-jp) REGION="Japan" ;;
              prod) REGION="US" ;;
              *) REGION="Unknown" ;;
            esac
            echo "export REGION='${REGION}'" >> "$BASH_ENV"
            echo "REGION: ${REGION}"

      - store_artifacts:
          path: playwright-report
          when: always

      - store_artifacts:
          path: test-results
          when: always

      - store_test_results:
          path: test-results

      # OASIS Integration: Convert JSON to Parquet and upload to OASIS
      - run:
          name: Convert report to Parquet and upload to OASIS
          when: always
          command: |
            if [ -f "playwright-report/results.json" ]; then
              # Validate TEST_ENV is set (provided via CircleCI context)
              if [ -z "${TEST_ENV}" ]; then
                echo "Warning: TEST_ENV not set, skipping OASIS upload"
                exit 0
              fi
              
              echo "Installing Python dependencies for OASIS upload..."
              # Playwright Docker image doesn't have pip - install it first
              apt-get update -qq && apt-get install -y -qq python3-pip >/dev/null 2>&1 || {
                echo "Warning: Failed to install pip, skipping OASIS upload"
                exit 0
              }
              
              # Install required packages
              python3 -m pip install --user --quiet --break-system-packages pandas pyarrow requests || {
                echo "Warning: Failed to install Python packages, skipping OASIS upload"
                exit 0
              }
              
              # All test results go to dev0 (Prod DevRev org)
              DEVREV_API_ENDPOINT="https://api.devrev.ai"
              echo "Using API endpoint: ${DEVREV_API_ENDPOINT} (dev0)"
              
              # Generate master timestamp for this execution
              MASTER_TIMESTAMP=$(date -u +"%Y%m%d_%H%M%S")
              echo "Master timestamp: ${MASTER_TIMESTAMP}"
              
              # Read region from file (set by login.setup.ts during test execution)
              REGION=$(cat region.txt 2>/dev/null || echo "unknown")
              echo "Detected region: ${REGION}"
              
              # Step 1: Convert Playwright JSON to Parquet
              echo "Converting Playwright report to Parquet format..."
              python3 scripts/oasis/convert_to_parquet.py \
                --report ./playwright-report/results.json \
                --output ./playwright-report/report.parquet \
                --environment "${TEST_ENV}" \
                --region "${REGION}" \
                --service "sentinel" \
                --version "${APP_VERSION:-unknown}" \
                --timestamp "${MASTER_TIMESTAMP}" \
                --test-type "regression" \
                || {
                  echo "Warning: Parquet conversion failed, skipping OASIS upload"
                  exit 0
                }
              
              echo "Parquet file created successfully"
              
              # Step 2: Upload to OASIS (requires service account token)
              if [ -n "${DEVREV_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN}" ]; then
                echo "Uploading to OASIS..."
                python3 scripts/oasis/upload_to_oasis.py \
                  --parquet ./playwright-report/report.parquet \
                  --dataset "sentinel_playwright_test_results" \
                  --dataset-title "Sentinel Playwright Test Results" \
                  --dataset-description "Automated E2E test results from Sentinel Playwright tests" \
                  --api-endpoint "${DEVREV_API_ENDPOINT}" \
                  || echo "Warning: OASIS upload failed, continuing..."
              else
                echo "DEVREV_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN not set, skipping OASIS upload"
                echo "Parquet file is still available in artifacts"
              fi
            else
              echo "No Playwright JSON report found, skipping OASIS integration"
            fi

  ########################################
  # JOBS - COMPUTER AUTOMATION
  ########################################

  computer-tests:
    docker:
      - image: mcr.microsoft.com/playwright:v1.56.0-noble
    resource_class: large

    steps:

      - restore_cache:

      - run:

      - save_cache:

      - run:

          no_output_timeout: 40m

          name: Export APP_VERSION, PLUG_VERSION and REGION for notifications

########################################
# WORKFLOWS
########################################

workflows:
  ########################################
  # WORKFLOWS - CI/CD (Git push/PR triggers)
  ########################################

  pull-request-checks:
    when:
      and:
        - not: << pipeline.parameters.run_manual_smoke_test >>
        - not: << pipeline.parameters.run_post_deployment_smoke_test >>
        - not: << pipeline.parameters.run_manual_regression_test >>
        - not: << pipeline.parameters.run_manual_computer_test >>
        - not: << pipeline.parameters.run_post_deployment_computer_test >>
    jobs:
      - lint:
          filters:
            branches:
              ignore:
                - develop
                - main
      - test:
          filters:
            branches:
              ignore:
                - develop
                - main

  protected-branch-validation:
    when:
      and:
        - not: << pipeline.parameters.run_manual_smoke_test >>
        - not: << pipeline.parameters.run_post_deployment_smoke_test >>
        - not: << pipeline.parameters.run_manual_regression_test >>
        - not: << pipeline.parameters.run_manual_computer_test >>
        - not: << pipeline.parameters.run_post_deployment_computer_test >>
    jobs:
      - lint:
          filters:
            branches:
              only:
                - develop
                - main
      - test:
          filters:
            branches:
              only:
                - develop
                - main

  ########################################
  # WORKFLOWS - SENTINEL SMOKE (SCHEDULED)
  ########################################

  # QA Environment - Daily at 07:00 & 19:00 IST
  qa-smoke-tests-0700:
    triggers:
